message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 21
Recent Green phase: No implementation change needed — the high-enchantment boundary test (amulet at exactly enchantment 5 → 89 G) was already green; threshold uses >= 5.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. If no refactoring is warranted, document why (the code has been refactored many times and may already be clean).

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 21 tests pass. The recent Green phase touched the high-enchantment threshold logic (the `>= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` rule at lines 87-89). Let me evaluate that area for refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing the recently-touched code and surrounding names:

- `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE` — clear, reveal intent
- `SURCHARGE_RULES`, `itemSurchargeRate`, `itemSurcharge`, `itemSurcharges` — clear, though `itemSurcharge`/`itemSurcharges` differ only by plural
- `sumApplicableRates`, `RateRule`, `applies`/`rate` — clear and general
- `formsBlock`, `typeBasePremium`, `policyBasePremium`, `policyModifierRate`, `applyModifierRate`, `roundUpPremium`, `quotePremium`, `runScenario` — all accurately describe their purpose

The names are accurate. No rename warranted.

## Step 2: Examine the recently-touched surcharge rule for duplication

Looking at lines 84-90, the `SURCHARGE_RULES` has an asymmetry the recent test highlighted. The curse rule reads `item.cursed === true` while the enchantment rule reads `(item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`. Both are concise and clear. The `?? 0` correctly handles the optional `enchantment` field (items like runes have none).

Let me look for genuine duplication. There's a conceptual pattern in `RateRule`-based collections: both `SURCHARGE_RULES` and `POLICY_MODIFIER_RULES` use `sumApplicableRates`. That's already DRY — the shared mechanism is extracted.

## Step 3: APP Mass — the recently-touched rule

Let me focus the mass analysis on the high-enchantment predicate, which the last Green phase confirmed:

```
applies: (item) => (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD
```

Mass: bindings `item`, `item.enchantment`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (3), constant `0` (1), conditional `??` (4), comparison `>=` (treated as invocation, 2) = **10**. This is minimal and readable. Rewriting to drop `?? 0` would require changing the `Item` type or risk `undefined >= 5` (which is `false` but obscures intent). Not worth it — Rule 2 (clarity) trumps the small mass saving.

## Evaluation: scanning the rest for a warranted improvement

The mandate is to attempt at least one refactoring. Let me check the one near-duplication I noted — `itemSurcharge` vs `itemSurcharges` naming. The singular/plural distinction is a common, legible convention (one item vs. a collection), so renaming would not improve clarity.

Checking `typeBasePremium` (lines 50-53):

```
const typeBasePremium = (type, count) => {
  if (formsBlock(type, count)) return BLOCK_PREMIUM;
  return count * BASE_PREMIUMS[type];
};
```

This early-return guard is clear. An expression form (`formsBlock(...) ? BLOCK_PREMIUM : ...`) would be equivalent mass and arguably less readable for a domain rule. No improvement.

The code is the product of many prior refactor cycles: a generic `RateRule<T>` mechanism shared by item surcharges and policy modifiers, well-named domain constants, extracted pure helpers, and explanatory comments on the non-obvious decisions (modifier scope, rounding direction, follow-up derivation). I genuinely could not find a change that improves naming, removes duplication, or reduces mass without sacrificing clarity.

Per the directive to attempt at least one refactoring, I considered several candidates concretely (dropping `?? 0`, ternary-izing `typeBasePremium`, merging `itemSurcharge`/`itemSurcharges`) and each would either leave mass unchanged or trade clarity for a marginal change. I will not apply a change that makes the code worse.

Let me do a final test run to confirm nothing changed and all remains green.

content.text:
Refactoring Complete

Refactoring: None applied — no improvement is warranted. The implementation has been refactored across many prior cycles and is already clean. I evaluated concrete candidates and rejected each because it would not improve the code:

- Naming (first priority): All names accurately reveal intent — `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, `SURCHARGE_RULES`, `policyModifierRate`, `roundUpPremium`, `runScenario`, etc. The `itemSurcharge`/`itemSurcharges` singular-vs-plural pair is a legible one-vs-collection convention, not confusing duplication. No rename improves clarity.
- Reveals intent (Rule 2): The recently-confirmed high-enchantment predicate `(item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` correctly handles items with no `enchantment` field (e.g. runes). Dropping `?? 0` would rely on `undefined >= 5` and obscure intent — a clarity loss. Non-obvious decisions (modifier scope, rounding direction, follow-up derivation) already carry explanatory comments.
- No duplication (Rule 3): The `RateRule<T>` + `sumApplicableRates` mechanism is already shared by both `SURCHARGE_RULES` and `POLICY_MODIFIER_RULES`. No remaining duplication to extract.
- Fewest elements (Rule 4): No unnecessary abstractions. The generic `RateRule<T>` earns its place by being reused twice.

APP mass (high-enchantment predicate, the last Green-phase area): bindings (3) + constant `0` (1) + conditional `??` (4) + comparison (2) = 10. Already minimal; the only mass-reducing rewrite (`?? 0` removal) sacrifices clarity, and Rule 2 trumps APP.

Mass change: unchanged (no edit made).
Tests: 21 passing, 24 todo — all green before and after.
